1: In that day seven women will seize one man — the demographic catastrophe after the military slaughter of Isaiah 3:25. The seven-to-one ratio suggests nearly total male death. The women's plea — let us use your name, only let us take away our reproach — is a reversal of the normal marriage contract: they offer to support themselves if they can only bear a husband's name and escape social shame. The reproach (herpah) is the stigma of unmarried or childless status. This desolation sets the stage: the community is at its lowest before the Branch appears. The Christological pattern: the Valley of Achor (trouble) becomes a door of hope (Hosea 2:15); the desolation of 4:1 becomes the occasion for the Branch of Isaiah 4:2.
2: In that day the Branch of YHWH (tsemach YHWH) will be beautiful and glorious — the most explicit messianic title in the early chapters of Isaiah. The tsemach (Branch/Shoot) becomes a technical messianic term in Jeremiah 23:5, Isaiah 33:15, Zechariah 3:8, Isaiah 6:12 — always pointing to the Davidic king who will reign in righteousness. Here tsemach YHWH (the Branch of YHWH himself) suggests more than a Davidic descendant: the Branch belongs to YHWH in a unique sense. The produce of the land being the pride and splendor of the survivors merges the messianic figure with the land's fruitfulness — the Davidic king and creation's restoration are inseparable. The NT identifies Jesus as the tsemach: the shoot from the stump of Jesse (Isaiah 11:1), the true vine (John 15:1), the firstfruits of the new creation (1 Corinthians 15:20-23).
3: Everyone who remains in Zion and is left in Jerusalem will be called holy — enrolled among the living. The remnant principle reaches its eschatological culmination: the survivors are designated qadosh (holy), the same term applied to YHWH's seraphim-proclaimed holiness (Isaiah 6:3). To be called holy is to be included in YHWH's own sphere of the sacred. The enrolled-among-the-living language anticipates the book of life (Revelation 20:12-15, Isaiah 21:27) — the final register of those who belong to the new Jerusalem. Peter applies the same language to the church: a holy nation (1 Peter 2:9), the eschatological community of those who remain.
4: The Lord will wash away the filth of the daughters of Zion and rinse the blood from the midst of Jerusalem by a spirit of judgment and a spirit of burning. The washing is YHWH's act, not Israel's self-purification. The spirit of judgment (ruach mishpat) and spirit of burning (ruach baer) are the two modes of the divine purification: forensic judgment that declares guilt and consuming fire that removes it. John the Baptist announces the same double purification: baptism with water and with fire and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 3:11-12). The burning of the chaff and the gathering of the wheat into the barn is the Christological fulfillment of this verse — the Son comes with a winnowing fork (Matthew 3:12).
5: Then YHWH will create over every dwelling-place on Mount Zion and over all her gatherings a cloud by day and smoke with the glow of flaming fire by night. The re-creation of the Sinai wilderness pillar of cloud and fire over the whole community, not just the tabernacle — the Shekinah presence that was localized in the tabernacle and temple is now extended to every household in Zion. This is the new-creation Pentecost: the Spirit-fire that rested on the single tabernacle becomes the Spirit poured out on all flesh (Joel 2:28-29, Acts 2:1-4). The tongues of fire over the assembled disciples in Acts 2:3 are the new-covenant fulfillment of this verse — the cloud and fire of divine presence descending on the whole assembly.
6: There will be a shelter for shade from the heat of the day, a refuge and hiding place from storm and rain. The shelter (sukkah) of the presence completes the restoration: the wilderness wandering's temporary shelter (the Feast of Sukkot) is now permanent. The shade from heat and refuge from storm echo the language of Ps 91 (he who dwells in the shelter of the Most High) and the Servant's description (Isaiah 32:2 — a man will be like a hiding place from the wind, a shelter from the storm). Jesus as the tabernacle dwelling of God (John 1:14, eskēnōsen en hēmin — pitched his tent among us) fulfills this promise: in him the shelter of God's presence is made permanent and universally accessible.
